@I322680433053@.name: Sir Thomas Boleyn, 1st Earl of Wiltshire
@I322680433053@.story: Thomas Boleyn, 1st Earl of Wiltshire and Ormond (c.1477-1539), of Hever Castle in Kent, was an English diplomat and politician at the court of Henry VIII. He was the father of Anne Boleyn, the King's second wife, and thus maternal grandfather of Queen Elizabeth I. Hever Castle was the seat of the Boleyn family from 1462 to 1539; the portrait link is the Boleyn family arms.

@I322738217996@.name: Lady Mary Boleyn, Countess of Wiltshire (Royal Mistress of Henry VIII)
@I322738217996@.story: Mary Boleyn (c.1499-1543) was the sister of Queen Anne Boleyn and is known to history as a mistress of King Henry VIII. The Boleyn family seat was Hever Castle in Kent. The portrait is a 17th-century copy attributed to Remigius van Leemput traditionally identified as Mary Boleyn.

@I322738217967@.name: Sir William Carey of Aldenham (courtier)
@I322738217967@.castle_name: Hever Castle (Boleyn family seat, his wife's home)
@I322738217967@.story: William Carey (c.1500-1528) was a courtier and favourite of King Henry VIII, serving as a Gentleman of the Privy Chamber. He married Mary Boleyn, the King's mistress and sister of Anne Boleyn; the Boleyn family seat was Hever Castle in Kent.

@I322738217959@.name: Lady Catherine Carey (Lady Knollys)
@I322738217959@.story: Catherine Carey (c.1524-1569), later Lady Knollys, was chief Lady of the Bedchamber to Queen Elizabeth I, who was her first cousin. She was the daughter of Mary Boleyn, sister of Queen Anne Boleyn, and married Sir Francis Knollys.

@I322738217958@.name: Sir Francis Knollys of Rotherfield Greys
@I322738217958@.story: Sir Francis Knollys, KG (c.1511-1596), of Rotherfield Greys, Oxfordshire, was an English courtier serving Henry VIII, Edward VI and Elizabeth I, and Treasurer of the Royal Household from 1572 until his death. He married Catherine Carey, first cousin of Queen Elizabeth I.

@I322668238131@.name: Sir Henry Norris, 1st Baron Norreys of Rycote
@I322668238131@.story: Henry Norris, 1st Baron Norreys of Rycote (1525-1601), was an English politician and diplomat from an old Berkshire family long established at the English court. His seat was Rycote in Oxfordshire; the surviving Rycote Chapel adjoins the lost great house.

@I322668238072@.name: Lady Margaret Williams, Baroness Norreys of Rycote
@I322668238072@.story: Margaret Williams (d.1599), heiress of Rycote, was the daughter of John Williams, 1st Baron Williams of Thame, and wife of Henry Norris, 1st Baron Norreys, bringing the Rycote estate in Oxfordshire to the Norreys family. No verified individual portrait exists; the image is of Rycote, the family seat.

@I322668238167@.name: Sir John Williams, 1st Baron Williams of Thame
@I322668238167@.story: John Williams, 1st Baron Williams of Thame (c.1500-1559), was Master of the King's Jewels and Lord President of the Council of the Welsh Marches, summoned to Parliament as Lord Williams of Thame in 1554. The image shows his elaborate tomb in St Mary's Church, Thame, Oxfordshire.

@I322668238192@.name: Lady Mary Fiennes (Baroness)
@I322668238192@.story: The Fiennes family (Lords Dacre) built and held Herstmonceux Castle in East Sussex, one of the oldest significant brick buildings in England, begun in 1441 by Sir Roger Fiennes. The image is the Fiennes family castle seat; no verified individual portrait exists for this person.

@I322738218154@.name: Sir Thomas Cary of Chilton, Baron Cary of Clovelly
@I322738218154@.story: The Cary (Carey) family of Devon held Clovelly Court in Clovelly, Devon. Thomas Cary of Chilton (associated with Clovelly) belonged to this gentry family; the image is Clovelly Court, the family seat. No verified individual portrait exists.

@I322482798704@.name: Sir John Thomas Warburton (of Arley)
@I322482798704@.story: The Warburton family were the historic owners of Arley Hall in Cheshire, holding the Arley estate for centuries before it passed to the Egerton-Warburton/Ashbrook line. The image is Arley Hall, the Warburton family seat. No verified individual portrait exists.

@I322530981723@.name: Sir Peter Warburton (of Arley)
@I322530981723@.story: Member of the Warburton family of Arley, Cheshire. Sir Peter Warburton (1542-1626) was a justice; the Warburton family seat was Arley Hall, shown here. No verified individual portrait exists.

@I322745596658@.name: Sir John Peter Warburton, Knight of Arley
@I322745596658@.story: Member of the Warburton family, hereditary owners of Arley Hall in Cheshire. The image is Arley Hall, the family seat. No verified individual portrait exists.

@I322745596662@.name: Lady Mary Brereton (m. Warburton)
@I322745596662@.story: Of the Brereton family of Cheshire, whose seat was Brereton Hall, an Elizabethan prodigy house near Brereton Green. The image is Brereton Hall, the family seat. No verified individual portrait exists.

@I322745596730@.name: Sir William Brereton
@I322745596730@.story: Of the prominent Brereton family of Cheshire; the family seat was Brereton Hall, shown here. (A William Brereton of this family, d.1536, was a courtier of Henry VIII executed in connection with Anne Boleyn.) No verified individual portrait for this entry.

@I322625934989@.name: William Molyneux, High Sheriff of Lancaster (of Sefton)
@I322625934989@.story: The Molyneux family of Sefton, Lancashire (later Earls of Sefton), held Croxteth Hall and the manor of Sefton. The image is Croxteth Hall, their ancestral home. No verified individual portrait exists.

@I322625934979@.name: Sir John William Mullins / Molyneux
@I322625934979@.story: Associated with the Molyneux (Mullins/Molines) family of Sefton, Lancashire, whose seat was Croxteth Hall. The image is the Molyneux family seat. No verified individual portrait exists.

@I322625939101@.name: Sir William Molyneux (of Sefton)
@I322625939101@.story: Of the Molyneux family of Sefton, Lancashire, ancestors of the Earls of Sefton. Sir William Molyneux (1471-1548) was a soldier who fought at Flodden. The family seat was Croxteth Hall, shown here. No verified individual portrait exists.

@I322625940387@.name: Lady Anne Parr, Countess of Pembroke
@I322625940387@.story: Anne Parr, Countess of Pembroke (1515-1552), was lady-in-waiting to all six wives of Henry VIII and the younger sister of his sixth queen, Katherine Parr. The Parr family seat was Kendal Castle in Westmorland, shown here. No verified individual portrait exists (the only candidate is a Holbein drawing of an unidentified woman).

@I322625940390@.name: Sir Thomas Parr of Kendal (father of Queen Catherine Parr)
@I322625940390@.story: Sir Thomas Parr of Kendal (1483-1517) was an English courtier and father of Katherine Parr, sixth wife of Henry VIII. The Parr family held Kendal Castle in Westmorland, shown here. The linked article concerns his wife Maud Green, Lady Parr; their joint tomb survives.

@I322668194797@.name: Lord Thomas Cromwell, 1st Earl of Essex
@I322668194797@.story: Thomas Cromwell, 1st Earl of Essex (c.1485-1540), was an English statesman and lawyer who served as chief minister to King Henry VIII from 1534 until 1540, engineering the English Reformation and the dissolution of the monasteries. He was beheaded on Henry's orders in 1540. The portrait is the famous Holbein-school image.

@I322668239337@.name: Elizabeth Moore, Baroness Cromwell, 1st Countess of Essex
@I322668239337@.story: Elizabeth (Wykys), wife of Thomas Cromwell, 1st Earl of Essex; through her marriage she is styled in family trees as Baroness Cromwell. The linked article is for her husband Thomas Cromwell, chief minister to Henry VIII. No verified individual portrait exists.

@I322625938260@.name: Louis XII, King of France (Duke of Orleans)
@I322625938260@.story: Louis XII (1462-1515), known as Louis of Orleans, was King of France from 1498 to 1515 and King of Naples from 1501 to 1504. He was born at the Royal Chateau of Blois in the Loire Valley, which became his principal residence and is shown here.

@I322625938264@.name: Anne of Brittany, Duchess of Brittany, Queen Consort of France
@I322625938264@.story: Anne of Brittany (1477-1514) was suo jure Duchess of Brittany and twice Queen Consort of France, married first to Charles VIII and then to Louis XII. The portrait is from her illuminated Grandes Heures by Jean Bourdichon; the Chateau de Blois was a principal royal residence of her and Louis XII.

@I322728996097@.name: Sir Anthony Browne, 1st Viscount Montagu
@I322728996097@.story: Anthony Browne, 1st Viscount Montagu (1528-1592), was an English peer of the Tudor period, a staunch Roman Catholic yet loyal to the Crown, employed on diplomatic missions to Rome and Spain and a commissioner at the trial of Mary, Queen of Scots. His seats included Cowdray House in Sussex (shown, now a ruin) and Battle Abbey. The portrait is by Hans Eworth.

@I322728996115@.name: Sir Anthony Browne (Knight of the Garter, Master of the Horse)
@I322728996115@.story: Sir Anthony Browne (c.1500-1548), Knight of the Garter and Master of the Horse to Henry VIII, was father of the 1st Viscount Montagu and received Battle Abbey in Sussex (shown) at the dissolution of the monasteries. The linked article covers his son the 1st Viscount Montagu. No verified individual portrait isolated for the father.

@I322745357141@.name: Sir John Baker of Sissinghurst ('Bloody Baker')
@I322745357141@.story: Sir John Baker (1488-1558) was Chancellor of the Exchequer (1545-1558) and Speaker of the House of Commons, nicknamed 'Bloody Baker' for his persecution of Protestants under Mary I. His country estate was Sissinghurst Castle in Kent, shown here.

@I322669833124@.name: Thomas 'Customer' Smythe of Westenhanger (and son Sir Thomas Smythe)
@I322669833124@.story: The Smythe family of Westenhanger Castle (Ostenhanger) in Kent rose to wealth under Thomas 'Customer' Smythe, collector of London customs. His son Sir Thomas Smythe (c.1558-1625), pictured, was the first governor of the East India Company and treasurer of the Virginia Company, a key organizer of the Jamestown settlement.

@I322625939476@.name: Sir Hugh Smyth of Long Ashton
@I322625939476@.story: The Smyth family were lords of Long Ashton in Somerset, holding the manor and its great house Ashton Court for four centuries. The image is Ashton Court, the Smyth family seat. No verified individual portrait exists.

@I322625940521@.name: Sir Basil Brooke (of Madeley)
@I322625940521@.story: Sir Basil Brooke (1576-1646) was an English metallurgist and Catholic recusant who inherited the manor of Madeley, Shropshire, with its ironworks and coal mines. His seat was Madeley Court, a 16th-century country house shown here.

@I322625941694@.name: Sir Robert Brooke (Broke), Justice of the Common Pleas
@I322625941694@.story: Sir Robert Broke (d.1558) was an English judge, Speaker of the House of Commons and legal writer, a landowner in Shropshire who acquired the manor of Madeley (later home to the Brooke family at Madeley Court). The portrait is the contemporary image of Sir Robert Broke.

@I322625941741@.name: Dame Anne Sapcote, Countess of Bedford
@I322625941741@.story: Anne Sapcote (d.1559) was an English noblewoman whose third marriage made her wife of John Russell, 1st Earl of Bedford. She was heiress of the Sapcote/Mordaunt lands in Northamptonshire; Drayton House (shown) is a great Northamptonshire house of the period. No verified individual portrait exists.

@I322738218200@.name: Lady Elizabeth Mordaunt
@I322738218200@.story: Of the Mordaunt family, Barons Mordaunt of Turvey, a leading Northamptonshire/Bedfordshire gentry and noble family. Drayton House in Northamptonshire (shown) is associated with the Mordaunt connection. No verified individual portrait exists.

@I322625936624@.name: Sir Robert Corbett (of Moreton Corbet)
@I322625936624@.story: Of the Corbet family of Moreton Corbet, Shropshire, whose seat was Moreton Corbet Castle, a medieval castle and Elizabethan manor still owned by the Corbet family. The image is the family castle. No verified individual portrait exists.

@I322625940001@.name: Sir John Corbet, 1st Baronet of Stoke
@I322625940001@.story: Of the Corbet family of Shropshire; the family's ancestral seat was Moreton Corbet Castle, shown here. No verified individual portrait exists.

@I322616879206@.name: Lady Alice Gage Browne
@I322616879206@.story: Of the Gage family of Firle, Sussex, who held Firle Place from the 15th century. Sir John Gage built Firle Place as his principal home and held high office under Henry VIII. The image is the Gage family seat. No verified individual portrait exists.

@I322746025851@.name: Lady Elizabeth Seton Gordon, Countess Marischal
@I322746025851@.story: Of the Gordon family, Earls of Huntly, whose ancestral home was Huntly Castle in Aberdeenshire, Scotland. The image is the Gordon family castle. No verified individual portrait exists.

@I322744641558@.name: Oliver Mainwaring of Windleshaw
@I322744641558@.story: Of the Mainwaring family of Cheshire, whose principal seat was Peover Hall at Over Peover. The image is the Mainwaring family seat. No verified individual portrait exists.

@I322625938315@.name: William Dryland Acton (of Aldenham)
@I322625938315@.story: Of the Acton family of Aldenham, near Morville in Shropshire, an ironmaster and MP family. Their seat, Aldenham Park (Aldenham Hall), is shown here. No verified individual portrait exists.

@I322710655849@.name: Sir William Dryland Acton, MP, ironmaster
@I322710655849@.story: Of the Acton family of Aldenham (Morville), Shropshire, ironmasters and MPs. The family seat Aldenham Park is shown here. No verified individual portrait exists.

@I322725936815@.name: Lady Ellen Dryland Acton
@I322725936815@.story: Of the Acton family of Aldenham (Morville), Shropshire. The family seat Aldenham Park is shown here. No verified individual portrait exists.

@I322743744992@.name: Sir Thomas Bacon (of Helmingham/Suffolk)
@I322743744992@.story: Of the Bacon family of Suffolk, the most famous member being Sir Nicholas Bacon (1510-1579), Lord Keeper of the Great Seal under Elizabeth I and father of Sir Francis Bacon (portrait shown). The Bacon family was seated in the Helmingham/Suffolk area; Helmingham Hall is the local moated manor. Portrait and seat are representative of the Bacon family.

@I322743745095@.name: Sir Michael John Bacon (Knight of Winston)
@I322743745095@.story: Of the Bacon family of Suffolk (Helmingham/Winston area). Helmingham Hall, the principal moated manor of the district, is shown. No verified individual portrait exists; the linked article is for the prominent Bacon kinsman Sir Nicholas Bacon.

@I322668194286@.name: Sir Edward Stradling, Lord of St Donats
@I322668194286@.story: Sir Edward Stradling (c.1474-1535) was knighted at Tournai in 1513 and was lord of St Donat's Castle in the Vale of Glamorgan, Wales. The Stradling family held St Donat's for four centuries. The image is the Stradling family castle. No verified individual portrait exists.

@I322668194093@.name: Lady Jane Stradling (Popham) of St Donats
@I322668194093@.story: Of the Stradling family of St Donat's Castle in Glamorgan, Wales. The image is the Stradling family seat. No verified individual portrait exists.

@I322681174171@.name: Sir William Mathew (Matthew) of Castell-y-Mynach
@I322681174171@.story: Of the Mathew family of Glamorgan, Wales, seated at Castell-y-Mynach. As no Wikipedia image exists for Castell-y-Mynach, the regional great castle of the Glamorgan/Monmouthshire gentry, Raglan Castle, is shown for context. No verified individual portrait exists.

@I322681174163@.name: Lady Alice Ragland
@I322681174163@.story: Named for Raglan (Ragland) in Monmouthshire, Wales, site of Raglan Castle, the great fortified residence of the Herbert and Somerset families. The image is Raglan Castle. No verified individual portrait exists.

@I322745627239@.name: Gruffudd ap John Jones (of Castellmarch)
@I322745627239@.story: Associated with Castellmarch in Caernarvonshire, Wales. No Wikipedia image exists for Castellmarch; Treowen, the most important gentry house in nearby Monmouthshire, is shown as a representative Welsh gentry seat of the era. No verified individual portrait exists.

@I322725937310@.name: William Jones, Lord of Hendre Obeth / Treowen
@I322725937310@.story: Of the Jones family of Treowen in Monmouthshire, Wales, an early-17th-century house regarded as the most important gentry house in the county. The image is Treowen, the family seat. No verified individual portrait exists.

@I322744489099@.name: Sir John Brydges, 1st Baron Chandos of Sudeley
@I322744489099@.story: John Brydges, 1st Baron Chandos of Sudeley (1491-1557), was an English courtier, army officer and politician prominent under Henry VIII, Edward VI and Mary I; as Lieutenant of the Tower he had custody of Lady Jane Grey. His seat was Sudeley Castle in Gloucestershire, shown here. (No verified standalone portrait; the article image is a Delaroche painting of Jane Grey.)

@I322744510266@.name: Lady Elizabeth de Grey, Baroness Chandos of Sudeley
@I322744510266@.story: Wife of John Brydges, 1st Baron Chandos, and so Baroness Chandos of Sudeley. The family seat was Sudeley Castle in Gloucestershire, shown here. No verified individual portrait exists; the linked article is for her husband.
